does: This paper demonstrates fundamental vulnerabilities in AI agent defenses against prompt injection attacks. The authors show that the dominant defense paradigm of data-instruction separation fails to detect attacks exploiting contextual manipulation while simultaneously degrading legitimate contextual behavior. They introduce three attack mechanisms: misrepresenting information flows, manipulating contextual norms, and mixing multiple flows. The work develops concrete benign and adversarial scenarios that force agents to violate contextual norms, revealing an impossibility result where defenders cannot simultaneously block all attacks and permit legitimate flows without contradictions.
connects: This work builds on prior research into prompt injection vulnerabilities that have demonstrated the fragility of deployed AI systems to adversarial inputs. It responds to existing defense approaches that attempt to strictly separate instructions from data, showing these methods are fundamentally limited. The paper draws on Contextual Integrity theory from privacy research, applying formal frameworks originally developed to analyze information-flow norms in human contexts to the AI safety domain. This bridges privacy-focused theoretical work with concrete AI security problems, extending beyond previous patch-based defenses that treated prompt injection as an isolated technical problem rather than a systemic architectural issue.
enables: This work opens new research directions by reframing prompt injection as a contextual integrity problem rather than a technical separation problem. It suggests future work should move beyond defensive patching toward systems that fundamentally respect and enforce contextual norms. The impossibility result motivates exploration of architectures that acknowledge irreducible tensions between security and functionality. This framework could inform design principles for more transparent AI agents that make norms explicit to users, potentially enabling better alignment between system behavior and user expectations. It also invites adoption of formal privacy and information-flow frameworks to AI security, suggesting stronger theoretical foundations for analyzing vulnerabilities in complex multi-context systems.
